1.pericope_label: Praise and a Plea for Help
1.synthesis: Psalm 40 moves in two registers: it opens with an extraordinary thanksgiving for past rescue and pivots to urgent lament, making it one of the most striking structural hybrid psalms in the Psalter. The opening declaration is of patient waiting rewarded: “I waited patiently for the Lord; he inclined to me and heard my cry. He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog, and set my feet upon a rock, making my steps secure” (Ps 40:1–2). A new song was put in his mouth; many would see and fear (40:3). The psalmist reflects on YHWH’s character: “You have multiplied, O Lord my God, your wondrous deeds and your thoughts toward us” (40:5). Then the pivotal declaration: “In sacrifice and offering you have not delighted, but you have given me an open ear. Burnt offering and sin offering you have not required. Then I said, ‘Behold, I have come; in the scroll of the book it is written of me: I delight to do your will, O my God; your law is within my heart’” (40:6–8). Righteousness has been proclaimed; steadfast love and faithfulness not concealed (40:9–10). But now the lament: evils without number surround him; his iniquities have overtaken him; he cannot see (40:12). The closing echoes Psalm 70 almost verbatim: “As for me, I am poor and needy, but the Lord takes thought for me. You are my help and my deliverer; do not delay, O my God!” (40:17).
1.historical_context: The central section of Psalm 40 (vv. 6–8) is among the most theologically loaded passages in the Psalter and is quoted extensively in Hebrews 10:5–7 as the words of Christ at his incarnation. The Hebrew of 40:6—“ears you have dug/opened for me”—is rendered in the Septuagint as “a body you have prepared for me,” and Hebrews follows the Septuagint in applying this to the Incarnation: Christ coming into the world as the one who does the Father’s will through a body prepared for sacrifice. The psalmist’s reference to a “scroll of the book” (40:7) in which something is written about him is interpreted in Hebrews as Christ’s reference to the whole Old Testament scriptures that “speak of me” (John 5:39). The second half of the psalm (vv. 13–17) is paralleled almost exactly by Psalm 70, suggesting either that Psalm 40 was composed by combining two originally separate poems or that Psalm 70 was excerpted from Psalm 40.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 40’s dual structure—past rescue/praise (vv. 1–10) and present lament/petition (vv. 11–17)—is not a structural flaw but a theological argument: past rescue does not immunize against present crisis, but it does provide the ground for present confidence. The psalmist who was once drawn from the pit is now in trouble again, yet the testimony of the first half is the basis for the petition of the second. The critical hinge is the declaration of willing obedience in vv. 6–8: between thanksgiving and lament stands the psalmist’s commitment to do God’s will, with the law in his heart. This is not moral self-commendation but the identification of the psalmist’s fundamental orientation—from which the surrounding crises cannot ultimately dislodge him.
1.theological_application: “I delight to do your will, O my God; your law is within my heart” (40:8) is the Psalter’s most direct anticipation of the new covenant promise in Jeremiah 31:33: “I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts.” The critique of sacrifice in 40:6–8 does not abolish the cult but locates it within a framework of obedience: what YHWH wants is not mere ritual compliance but the delighted conformity of the heart to his will. Communities shaped by this psalm learn to ask not merely whether they are performing the required religious acts but whether the law has moved from the external (scroll, ritual) to the internal (the heart). The past rescue from the pit (40:2) is the motive for present obedience, and present obedience is the context from which new petition arises.
1.christology: Hebrews applies Ps 40:6–8 directly to Christ: “When Christ came into the world, he said, ‘Sacrifices and offerings you have not desired, but a body have you prepared for me’” (Heb 10:5–7). The Incarnation is the moment of this declaration: the eternal Son taking a body to do what animal sacrifices could not—actually remove sin through the one perfect act of self-offering. “Behold, I have come to do your will” is the logic of the cross: Christ coming as the willing servant who replaces the temple’s repeated offerings with a once-for-all sacrifice (Heb 10:10). The new song put in the psalmist’s mouth (40:3) is the song of redemption that creation joins when the Lamb is declared worthy: “Worthy are you to take the scroll and to open its seals, for you were slain” (Rev 5:9).
